document.title: Conventional versus digital radiographs for intraoperative cervical spine-level localization: a prospective time and cost analysis

sourceSummary.oneLine: Prospective nonrandomized clinical study comparing conventional versus digital radiography for intraoperative cervical spine-level localization during single-level ACDF.
sourceSummary.officialUse: Clinical literature evidence for digital radiography use in intraoperative localization; the article states FDA device/drug status is not applicable.
keyPoints: Eighteen patients were enrolled: ten underwent localization with conventional radiography and eight with digital imaging.
Mean localization time was 823 seconds for conventional radiography and 100 seconds for digital radiography, with p<.001.
The authors concluded digital imaging provides equally accurate information as conventional radiography in significantly reduced time.
The article states image quality, archival ease, and manipulation provided by digital radiography are superior to fluoroscopy.
FDA device/drug status is stated as not applicable.

concepts.name: Digital radiography
concepts.evidence: Either a conventional or a digital radiography was used in each case.

concepts.name: Conventional radiography
concepts.evidence: Ten patients underwent localization with conventional radiography, whereas eight patients underwent localization with digital imaging.

concepts.name: Intraoperative cervical spine-level localization
concepts.evidence: To prospectively evaluate the time and operating room efficiency differences between the two methods for intraoperative level localization.

concepts.name: Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion
concepts.evidence: Prospective consecutive patients undergoing a single-level anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) with plate and allograft.

concepts.name: Fluoroscopy
concepts.evidence: Fluoroscopy permits real-time localization of the spinal level but is limited by decreased resolution compared with conventional or digital radiography.

evidence.claim: The document is a clinical study comparing conventional and digital radiographs for intraoperative cervical spine-level localization.
evidence.evidenceText: Clinical Study Conventional versus digital radiographs for intraoperative cervical spine-level localization: a prospective time and cost analysis
evidence.pageTarget: Clinical study of intraoperative cervical spine localization radiography

evidence.claim: The study design was prospective and nonrandomized.
The patient sample was consecutive patients undergoing single-level ACDF with plate and allograft.
evidence.evidenceText: PATIENT SAMPLE: Prospective consecutive patients undergoing a single-level anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) with plate and allograft.

evidence.claim: Eighteen patients were enrolled, with ten conventional radiography cases and eight digital imaging cases.
evidence.evidenceText: Eighteen patients were enrolled in this study. Ten patients underwent localization with conventional radiography, whereas eight patients underwent localization with digital imaging.
evidence.pageTarget: Clinical study enrollment and imaging groups

evidence.claim: Mean localization time was 823 seconds for conventional radiography and 100 seconds for digital radiography, with statistically significant difference.
evidence.evidenceText: The mean time for conventional radiography was 823 seconds (standard deviation [SD], 159), and for digital, it was 100 seconds (SD, 34; p!.001).
evidence.pageTarget: Digital versus conventional radiography localization time

evidence.claim: The authors concluded digital imaging provides equally accurate information as conventional radiography in less time.
evidence.evidenceText: Digital imaging provides equally accurate information as conventional radiography in a significantly reduced amount of time.

evidence.claim: The article states FDA device/drug status is not applicable.
The article states digital radiography has better image quality, manipulation, and archiving than fluoroscopy.
evidence.evidenceText: Digital radiography provides better image quality, easier manipulation (eg, after processing), and archiving when compared with fluoroscopy.

evidence.claim: The authors state the study was not designed to assess true cost savings.
evidence.evidenceText: Lastly, the study was not designed and did not assess true cost savings.

evidence.claim: The journal citation is The Spine Journal volume 9, 2009, pages 967-971.
